content.assessment: Grade-splitting directly targets one documented equity harm — long commute burden falling hardest on lower-income households — by keeping younger, more vulnerable children local while consolidating older pupils where specialist teacher access can be improved. However, this relocates rather than eliminates sorting: it merely shifts the point of segregation risk to the upper-grade transition, where church schools' greater selection freedom could siphon off higher-status pupils, echoing the known intake gap between church and public schools.
opportunities: Could be designed with catchment safeguards to prevent the upper-grade consolidation point from becoming a new segregation chokepoint
strengths: Reduces commute burden specifically for younger children, who evidence shows are most vulnerable to the documented harms of long rural bus rides (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)

Consolidating upper grades could improve access to qualified specialist teachers, partially addressing the acute rural teacher shortage and aging workforce documented nationally (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09)
threats: Church-sector schools, which already show a more advantaged intake profile, may selectively absorb upper-grade pupils at the point of transition, intensifying rather than reducing segregation (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
Older children moved to consolidated centers still face commute burdens, and if bus infrastructure is inadequate the equity benefit is only partially realized (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)
weaknesses: Does not resolve the underlying between-settlement segregation dynamic; it only changes where the sorting point occurs, from village-level to upper-grade transition (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)

Village-level socioeconomic conditions are heterogeneous even among schooled villages (better demographics, worse employment/welfare), so grade-splitting is unlikely to have uniform equity effects across the varied village typology (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06)
